a: An eval (evaluation) is a test that checks how good a language model's outputs are. You give the model some inputs, look at what it produces, and score whether the answers are correct, helpful, or safe.
fs: An eval is a repeatable test that scores model outputs against a quality bar, so you can measure quality instead of just eyeballing a few answers.
fj: Treating a couple of hand-checked examples as proof it works, instead of running a consistent scored test you can repeat.

a: Ground truth is the known-correct answer you compare the model's output against. It is the reference you trust, so you can tell whether the model got it right or wrong.
fs: Ground truth is the trusted reference answer for an input; scoring compares the model's output to it to decide pass or fail.
fj: Assuming the model's own answer is the ground truth, instead of using an independently verified correct answer to grade it.

a: A golden dataset is a curated set of test cases, each with an input and its correct answer, that you run your evals against. It is your trusted yardstick for measuring quality over time.
fs: A golden dataset is a hand-checked collection of inputs plus expected answers covering common cases, edge cases, and known failures.
fj: Thinking more examples is always better; a small set of carefully verified cases beats a huge pile of unchecked ones.

a: LLM-as-a-judge means using another language model to grade an output. You give the judge model a rubric, like 'is this answer polite and on-topic?', and it scores the response for you when there is no single exact correct answer.
fs: LLM-as-a-judge uses a model with a clear rubric to score outputs at scale, handling fuzzy qualities like tone and helpfulness that code cannot easily check.
fj: Trusting the judge blindly; it can be biased or wrong, so you calibrate it against human ratings before relying on its scores.

q: What is a regression test (for LLMs)?
a: A regression test re-runs your evals after a change, like a new prompt or a new model, to make sure nothing that used to work got worse. If scores drop, you caught a regression before users do.
fs: A regression test compares new eval scores against the previous version on the same dataset, flagging any drop so changes do not quietly break things.
fj: Shipping a prompt or model change because it fixed one case, without re-checking the whole dataset for things it may have broken.
